_readme: Copy this entire folder, rename it (e.g. ch02), then edit this file. Delete any _readme fields before building.
title: Always On: Your High-Alert Brain
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Hypervigilance kept you alive. Now it is exhausting your life.

sections.heading: The Brain That Never Clocks Out

content.text: High-alert mode is not a personality flaw. It is a trained survival state. After repeated exposure to danger, your threat system learns to scan first and relax never.
The problem is that the same system that protects you on scene can start running your home life, sleep, and relationships.
What it looks like day to day

items: You sit with your back to walls and track exits automatically.
Small noises trigger a full body jolt.
You feel exhausted but cannot power down.
People read you as angry when you are actually on guard.

lines: Hypervigilance is an over-trained alarm, not a character defect.

content.text: Your nervous system does not care about your address. It cares about signals. If your body still reads noise, surprise, and uncertainty as threat, home can feel like another mission.

items: No transition ritual between shift and home
High household noise and unpredictable demands

lines: Why this works: repeated orienting and grounding drills increase parasympathetic recovery and lower threat-bias in neutral environments.

sections.heading: Field Tools to Turn the Dial Down

content.text: Use tools that work in under two minutes. Do not wait until you are fully flooded.

items.text: say, 'I am in high alert,' to move from autopilot into choice.

items.lead: Drop your shoulders and lengthen exhale:

items.text: scan the room for five neutral objects to signal present-time safety.
20-60 seconds of movement burns off activation chemistry.

lines: Rule: the best reset is the one you will actually use during a rough day.

content.text: Families do better when high-alert has a shared language. Without a plan, everyone personalizes your stress response.

items: Agree on a signal phrase: 'I am yellow, I need 20 minutes.'
Use approach warnings instead of surprise contact from behind.
Set a predictable re-entry routine after shifts.
Repair quickly after sharp tone or withdrawal.

sections.heading: When Hypervigilance Becomes a Safety Risk

content.text: Escalate support when the alarm is running your life more than you are.

items: Reactivity is affecting parenting or partner safety.
You avoid routine places because your body treats them as threat zones.
You are stuck in constant irritability, shutdown, or anger spikes.

content.text: For one week, track one trigger, one body cue, and one reset action each day. Improvement is faster recovery, not zero activation.

lines: Run one reset before you hit red.
Complete one repair if your stress leaked onto others.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Always On - High-Alert Pattern Map
worksheets.intro: Use this to identify where high-alert helps and where it costs you.

question_groups.label: Where did hypervigilance activate this week?
question_groups.hint: List scenes at work, transitions, and home moments where your body stayed on guard.

question_groups.label: What did it cost in that moment?
question_groups.hint: Body strain, sleep disruption, conflict, emotional distance, or decision errors.

question_groups.label: What reset tool will you rehearse daily?
question_groups.hint: Choose one: long exhale, orienting scan, movement burst, cold-water reset.

numbered_prompts.hint: What cue flipped you into scan-and-defend mode?
Where did you feel it first: jaw, chest, gut, shoulders, breath?
How long until your body returned to baseline?
Write exact words or actions you will use next time.

worksheets.closing_note: Your win is catching activation earlier and recovering faster.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Always On - Partner/Family Check-In
worksheets.intro: Use this with a partner or trusted person to build a shared plan around high-alert days.

question_groups.label: What does my high-alert look like from the outside?
question_groups.hint: Ask for observable behavior only: tone, scanning, withdrawal, restlessness.

question_groups.label: What helps me come down, and what makes it worse?
question_groups.hint: List support actions and escalation triggers clearly.

question_groups.label: What is our agreement for the next high-alert moment?
question_groups.hint: Define signal phrase, time-to-return, and repair step.

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means pause and reset right now?
How long until we re-engage after the pause?
Write the exact sentence to use if activation caused damage.

worksheets.closing_note: High-alert is manageable when everyone follows the same playbook.

paragraphs: If your brain has been in guard mode for years, you are not broken. You are conditioned.
Conditioning can be retrained. Start with one reset tool and one relationship repair this week.
closing.highlight: You do not have to feel calm to act skillfully. You only need to catch the pattern sooner.
